content:
A Mother and Daughter Suicide Tragedy Made Me Rethink Education

My discovery led me to a system that builds a child’s brain and mind

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

School is meant to be a place where young minds come together to explore, listen, and grow. (Photo by Quilia on Unsplash)Recently, a heartbreaking tragedy happened in Hong Kong. A mother lost her life after jumping from her apartment following an argument with her daughter about education. Later at night, the 12-year-old daughter jumped from the apartment as well.
I am not a parent, nor am I an education expert. But this tragedy forced me to think: What is happening to our education system?
It made me rethink the true purpose of education. School should help children build a cognitive logical mind while nurturing an ethical, empathetic, curious, and resilient mind. Yet, our system skews toward relatively rote knowledge transfer and meritocracy, creating high academic pressure on students, parents, and teachers. Grades and certificates have become the ultimate goal of education. Learning has become a competition.

While looking for answers, I noticed that Norway is banning the use of AI in schools. This prompted me to dig deeper into their system. I love these words from the Prime Minister of Norway, Jonas Gahr Støre, when explaining their policy on education:
“We are introducing this legislation because we want a childhood where children get to be children.”
“Play, friendships, and everyday life must not be taken over by algorithms and screens.”
“The most important thing in school is that our children learn to read, write and do mathematics.”
What I admire most is that their education system is rooted in the Nordic philosophy concept of Bildung, which means all-round development. They believe school should be a thorough, intentional building project designed to help children shape their moral character, emotional resilience, and civic responsibility, rather than merely a mechanism for finding a good job.

Building Mind and Brain Through Education Reform
Driven by this philosophy, the government implemented The Knowledge Promotion Reform (LK20) in 2020. This framework legally mandates the integration of 6 Core Values and 3 Interdisciplinary Topics into every subject across all grades.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One of the objectives of education is to engage students to interact with teachers and peers. (Photo by CDC on Unsplash)The 6 Core Values

• Human dignity: Recognizing the worth of every individual.

• Identity and cultural diversity: Helping students know themselves and respect others.

• Critical thinking and ethical awareness: Training the brain to question information.

• The joy of creating, engagement, and the urge to explore: Fostering curiosity.

• Respect for nature and environmental awareness: Connecting deeply with the world.

• Democracy and participation: Learning to actively participate in society.
The 3 Interdisciplinary Topics
Instead of teaching these vital skills in separate, easily forgotten weekly seminars, teachers are legally required to weave these 3 core topics into daily lessons like math, language, and science:

• Health and Life Mastery: Teaching emotional regulation, stress management, and physical/mental resilience.

• Democracy and Citizenship: Teaching kids how to handle disagreements, respect boundaries, and prevent bullying.

• Sustainable Development: Helping kids understand their place in the environment and society.
Looking at this integration, I realized how much our own systems miss by treating these vital human skills as optional afterthoughts rather than the core foundation of every single lesson.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Education should be treated as a holistic project to shape both the hardware of the brain and the moral landscape of the mind. (Photo by Wiki Sinaloa on Unsplash)Outdoor Learning
And under the LK20 curriculum, there are outdoor learning lessons, often one day per week. They use the natural world as a dynamic laboratory to teach standard subjects. For example, a science lesson involves collecting real data on local biodiversity. They also teach outdoor survival and nature literacy. For example, at age 15 (Grade 10), students must independently plan and execute multi-day outdoor trips, cook over open fires, practice advanced first aid, and master wilderness survival.
No Official Grades
And there is one thing I really like: schools don't give official grades until age 13, which supports learning without pressure and fosters a mindset that mistakes are simply a natural part of the learning process. I think this is how a resilient mind is built.

Reclaiming the Classroom from Screens and AI
The Norwegian government is also very conscious of how digital devices, generative AI, and social media affect a child’s cognitive development.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Slowing down the digital world to reclaim a child’s attention span and mental well-being. (Photo by Saif71.com on Unsplash)The Smart Phone Ban: Improve Engagement and Well-being
In 2024, the Government implemented a smartphone ban in primary and secondary classrooms to stop digital distractions. The result has been encouraging: bullying decreased, grades improved, and student mental health rebounded.
Generative AI Ban: Protecting Thinking and Curiosity
Introducing AI too early hinders students’ ability to build core skills. Childhood is an important period for shaping how they approach problem-solving. When AI does the work, students miss out on vital cognitive development that comes from working through a struggle.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Is AI helping students to learn or hindering their ability to build core skills and curiosity? (Photo by Nahrizul Kadri on Unsplash)Instead, the system prioritizes fundamental skills: reading to decode text and understand the content; mathematics to master procedures; and writing to train in constructing arguments and organizing ideas. To protect foundational learning skills, Norway established a strict ban on generative AI. This approach made me think about how easily we rush to hand children the latest technology, forgetting that they need time to build their own cognitive skills before outsourcing them to AI.

• Ages 6 to 13 (Grades 1–7): Students are barred from using generative AI tools to ensure they learn to read, write, and do math independently.

• Ages 14 to 16 (Lower Secondary): Students may use AI tools, but only under direct teacher supervision.

• Ages 17 to 19 (Upper Secondary): Students are taught how to use AI appropriately to prepare them for the modern workforce and higher education.
Bring Back Physical Books and Handwriting
Moreover, the government is proposing legislation to fund more physical books in classrooms, completely reversing their tech-first policies that adopted computers in the 1990s and tablets in the 2010s.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Flipping physical pages and writing by hand creates the necessary neurological pathways for deep learning. (Photo by CDC on Unsplash)They realized that overreliance on screens and digital shortcuts was eroding children’s attention spans, reading comprehension, and fine motor skills.

Get KL Wu’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

By investing in more physical books and prioritizing handwriting over typing, they are restoring cognitive benefits. Flipping the pages improves memory retention and spatial mapping, while handwriting accelerates language processing. To protect their mental health, the government also plans to ban children from using social media until age 16.
As someone who used to read on screens every day for work, shifting to physical books and drafting my content flow by hand before typing it on Medium has marked a powerful shift. I have personally felt a profound difference in my memory, critical thinking, and comprehension.
A Different Look at Tech Readiness
Some might argue that delaying a child’s exposure to AI will cause them to fall behind in a tech-driven world. But I think it instead prepares them to use technology more ethically and intelligently later on.
When children first sharpen their foundational reading, writing, and critical thinking skills through physical books and paper, they are training their brains to construct deep arguments and organize complex ideas. This allows them to write sharper prompts and ask the right questions, and make the right judgment of an AI’s answer. They learn to view technology as a powerful tool to accelerate research and analysis, rather than a total replacement for their own brain.

The whole education system in Norway is so thoughtful because it takes care of both the brain and the mind's development. A brilliant brain is useless if the mind is paralyzed by anxiety, depression, and low self-worth. And the most effective way to learn resilience is through real, daily-life experience and genuine human interaction in the classroom, not through rote memorization or theory transfer.
I am very impressed by this framework. Every single measure is designed to create a protected environment for students to learn, not a place for them to compete. This is what a real education is about.
What about you? Do you think we should shift our expectations away from competitive grades and certificates if it means truly protecting a child’s mental well-being and building a stronger mind?
